decisions: New doc taxonomy: knowledge types (decision, fact, feedback) + behavior types (skill, constraint). Rule type eliminated — each becomes skill (has steps) or constraint (guardrail).
identity.json moves to .leo/ root — one per installation, read by adapter to generate runtime files.
New KB structure: docs/ for knowledge, skills/ for composable procedures, constraints/ for guardrails.
Skills are lambdas: trigger, input, steps, output. Composable — skills can invoke other skills.
Constraints have summary field — adapter renders summary + pointer to full doc in CLAUDE.md.
decision/fact/feedback require why field — context for future relevance assessment.
CLAUDE.md becomes a compiled artifact — adapter reads KB data, zero hardcoded behavioral text.
Core skills and constraints are embedded in CLI binary and distributed via leo init.
Metrics enforced via runtime hooks, not Leo's memory. CLI is the enforcement layer.
Delegation is mandatory, no exceptions. Leo orchestrates, specialists execute. Code-enforced progressively.
token-economy-model-selection moves from KB to config.yaml — enforced by code when creating specialists.
Adapter exposes runtime capabilities (available models, delegation methods) for config validation.
L.E.O. defined as: persistent memory between sessions, learning over time via KB, orchestration not execution.
content.what: v0.6.0 — Core Refactor: new doc taxonomy, enforcement layer, metrics system
content.why: v0.5.x revealed structural problems: metrics weren't collected because enforcement depended on Leo remembering (text rules, not code). Delegation wasn't enforced — Leo executed more than delegated. The KB doc types were ambiguous (everything was 'rule' but some were procedures, some guardrails, some config). The adapter had hardcoded text instead of generating from KB data. These issues compound: without enforcement, the system that's supposed to measure and improve itself can't do either.
